narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you bring from your background. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right around the average for students in our dataset, reflecting solid academic effort throughout your studies. However, your LSAT score of 23 is notably lower than the typical 36.7 average, which played the biggest role in the model's assessment of your bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 school decile of 3 and Year 3 decile of 4 are both below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively, adding some concern about early and later performance consistency. Coming from a middle-income family aligns with many applicants, while planning full-time studies matches the norm for over 90% of students evaluated. Overall, the model predicts a 72.6% chance you'll face challenges passing the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize those with stronger predicted odds of success. The LSAT score had the strongest pull toward this failure prediction, far outweighing other factors. Your undergraduate GPA offered the next biggest counterbalance, nudging the prediction toward passing, while your lower Year 1 decile pushed it back toward risk. Family income and full-time status had smaller influences, with the former slightly raising concerns and the latter mildly supportive. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile stands out for that lower LSAT and early deciles, which tipped the scales despite your average GPA. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening those early academic deciles through additional coursework, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas—many students like you succeed with targeted improvements. We're rooting for your success in law.
